title: Stargazing with Dad
summary: A boy and his dad lie on the warm desert ground in Australia and look up at the stars.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: Dad, are you ready? It is getting dark.

body.speaker: Dad
body.text: Yes, mate. Bring the warm blanket. The desert is cold at night.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: Wow, look! There are so many stars!

body.speaker: Dad
body.text: Yes. In the city we cannot see them. Here in the desert the sky is open.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: What is that big white line in the sky?

body.speaker: Dad
body.text: That is the Milky Way. It is made of millions of stars.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: Millions? That is a lot. Do they have names?

body.speaker: Dad
body.text: Some do. Look low and to the right. That bright one is called Sirius.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: Sirius. I like that name. Is it close?

body.speaker: Dad
body.text: No, mate. It is very far. The light from it takes nine years to reach us.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: Nine years! So I am looking at very old light.

body.speaker: Dad
body.text: Yes, you are. You are a little time traveller.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: Dad, look! A star is moving!

body.speaker: Dad
body.text: That is not a star. That is a small space station. Many people work inside.

body.speaker: Tom
body.text: Can we sleep here under the stars?

body.speaker: Dad
body.text: Yes. We will sleep here tonight. Hold my hand. The desert sky is the best blanket.

annotations.exampleEn: We went stargazing on the farm last night.
She put a warm blanket on the baby.
We can see the Milky Way far from the city.
The sky was full of millions of stars.
The full moon is very bright.

annotations.meaningVi: sao Thiên Lang (sao sáng nhất bầu trời đêm)
annotations.exampleEn: Sirius is the brightest star at night.
The letter will reach us next week.
He read a story about a time traveller.
Astronauts live in the space station.
Hold my hand when we cross the road.
Your love is the best blanket.

grammarNotes.title: 'There is / there are' for things you can see
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Khi chỉ ra thứ gì đó trên bầu trời hay xung quanh, ta dùng 'There is/are': 'There are so many stars!' Hai dạng phổ biến: 'There is + danh từ số ít', 'There are + danh từ số nhiều'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: To point things out, use 'There is/are': 'There are so many stars!' Use 'There is' with a singular noun and 'There are' with a plural noun.

grammarNotes.title: 'It takes + thời gian + to + động từ'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cấu trúc 'It takes + khoảng thời gian + to + động từ' nói về thời gian cần để làm việc gì: 'The light from it takes nine years to reach us.' Đây là cấu trúc rất hay dùng để nói về thời gian di chuyển, nấu ăn, học hành, v.v.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'It takes + time + to + verb' to say how long something needs: 'The light from it takes nine years to reach us.' This pattern is common for travel, cooking, and learning times.

translationVi: Tom: Bố ơi, bố sẵn sàng chưa? Trời đang tối rồi đó. Bố: Rồi đó con. Lấy cái chăn ấm đi. Sa mạc ban đêm lạnh lắm. Tom: Ồ, nhìn kìa! Sao nhiều quá! Bố: Ừ. Ở thành phố mình không thấy được sao. Ở sa mạc, bầu trời rất rộng. Tom: Cái vệt trắng to trên trời kia là gì vậy bố? Bố: Đó là Dải Ngân Hà. Nó được tạo bởi hàng triệu ngôi sao. Tom: Hàng triệu á? Nhiều thật. Sao có tên không bố? Bố: Có một số có tên. Con nhìn về phía thấp bên phải. Ngôi sao sáng đó tên là Sirius. Tom: Sirius. Con thích cái tên đó. Nó có gần không bố? Bố: Không đâu con. Nó rất xa. Ánh sáng từ nó phải mất chín năm mới tới được chỗ mình. Tom: Chín năm! Vậy con đang nhìn ánh sáng rất cũ rồi. Bố: Đúng rồi đó. Con đang là một nhà du hành thời gian nhỏ. Tom: Bố ơi, nhìn kìa! Có ngôi sao đang di chuyển! Bố: Cái đó không phải sao. Đó là một trạm vũ trụ nhỏ. Có nhiều người làm việc bên trong. Tom: Mình ngủ ngoài trời dưới bầu trời sao luôn được không bố? Bố: Được. Tối nay mình ngủ ở đây. Nắm tay bố nào. Bầu trời sa mạc là tấm chăn đẹp nhất.

questions.prompt: What does Dad ask Tom to bring?
Why is it hard to see stars in the city?
options: There is too much light, the sky is not open like the desert
questions.explanation: 'In the city we cannot see them. Here in the desert the sky is open.'

questions.prompt: What is the big white line in the sky?
What is the Milky Way made of?
questions.explanation: 'It is made of millions of stars.'

questions.prompt: What is the name of the bright star Dad shows?
How long does the light from Sirius take to reach us?
questions.explanation: 'The light from it takes nine years to reach us.'

questions.hint: Find what Dad says about Tom.

questions.prompt: What is the moving 'star' Tom sees?
Who works inside that moving thing?

questions.explanation: 'Can we sleep here under the stars?' Dad says yes.

questions.prompt: What does Dad call the sky?
questions.explanation: 'The desert sky is the best blanket.'

questions.prompt: What is the feeling at the end of the dialogue?
questions.explanation: Tom and Dad share a quiet night and hold hands.

Dad: Yes, mate. Bring the warm {{b2}}. The desert is cold at night.
Tom: Wow, look! There are so many {{b3}}!
Dad: Yes. In the city we cannot see them. Here in the desert the sky is open.
Tom: What is that big white line in the sky?
Dad: That is the {{b4}} Way. It is made of millions of stars.
Tom: Millions? That is a lot. Do they have names?
Dad: Some do. Look low and to the right. That {{b5}} one is called Sirius.
Tom: Sirius. I like that name. Is it close?
Dad: No, mate. It is very {{b6}}. The light from it takes nine {{b7}} to reach us.
Tom: Nine years! So I am looking at very old light.
Dad: Yes, you are. You are a little time {{b8}}.
Tom: Dad, look! A star is {{b9}}!
Dad: That is not a star. That is a small space {{b10}}. Many people work inside.
Tom: Can we sleep here under the stars?
Dad: Yes. We will sleep here tonight. Hold my {{b11}}. The desert sky is the best {{b12}}.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Why do you think people feel calm when they look at the stars? When was the last time you stopped to look at the sky?
